The Edmonds City Council postponed the vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator until next week. Council President Michelle Dotsch stated the high-profile position requires more public and council consideration.

Gayla Shoemake, environmental advocate and educator, was named Edmonds Kiwanis Club's Citizen of the Year. The award recognizes her decades of climate advocacy including leading the Edmonds Climate Advisory Board and installing solar panels at Edmonds United Methodist Church.
A Washington Supreme Court commissioner declined to pause a judge's ruling blocking stricter sheriff qualifications, keeping current eligibility standards in effect during ongoing litigation.
Seattle Pro Musica has concluded its 2025-26 auditions, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list for August 2026 all-voice-part auditions.

Edmonds Library (650 Main St) offers Baby Storytime with Kat on Tuesday, May 12 at 8:00–8:45 am and Preschool Storytime on Wednesday, May 27 at 10:00–10:45 am. Lynnwood Library lists Baby Storytime with Heather on Friday, May 8 at 9:30 am and Toddler Storytime on Tuesday, May 12 at 11:00–11:30 am.

US-Iran war enters critical phase: Trump claims a 14-point memorandum to end hostilities is close, but simultaneously threatens 'much higher level' bombing if Iran rejects it. Iran's foreign ministry says the US proposal is 'still being considered' while a senior MP dismisses it as a 'wish list'.

Israel resumes strikes on Beirut: Netanyahu orders targeted killing of senior Hezbollah commander in Dahieh suburb, first Israeli air strike on Lebanon's capital since April ceasefire. US Central Command confirms it disabled an Iranian-flagged oil tanker in Gulf of Oman as naval blockade continues.

Australia reels from Indigenous girl's death: Vigils planned nationwide for 5-year-old Aboriginal girl found dead in Perth, sparking clashes between protesters and police. The case has reignited debate over systemic discrimination and police treatment of Indigenous communities.

Global oil markets volatile: Brent crude surged past $100/barrel on Iran war fears before easing on ceasefire speculation. EU approves €90bn loan to Ukraine as Druzhba pipeline reopens, ending months-long deadlock over oil supplies to Hungary.

UK-France strike £662m migration deal: Three-year agreement includes deployment of 50 riot-trained French police to tackle Channel crossings and 'hostile crowds'. The deal comes amid record small boat arrivals and rising political pressure on both governments.

Sean Irving's essay 'The West's forgotten republican heritage' argues that 19th-century economic republicanism offers a roadmap for restoring citizens' agency in an age of corporate and algorithmic domination.
In 'The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You', Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks's posthumous essays into a meditation on perception as creative act rather than passive reception.

ProPublica's investigation 'Children Describe Life Inside Dilley ICE Detention Center' reconstructs the emotional geography of family incarceration through the voices of 14-year-old Ariana Velasquez and other children held at the Texas facility reopened under Trump's second-term deportation drive.

Construction on the 2025 sewer/storm pipe rehabilitation project begins Monday, May 4. The $934K CIPP (Cured-in-Place Pipe) program will replace aging mains on 242nd St SW, 5th Ave S, Bowdoin Way, 88th Ave W and ten other segments.

triadic_ontology.findings: Three recent papers advance triadic relational metaphysics: Isabelle proposes Triadic Relational Ontology (TRO) arguing that dyads alone cannot sustain emergence; a relational field must be actively maintained. Hammat offers a triadic neurophenomenology uniting material objects, mental ideas, and axiological relations through consciousness, with testable neural signatures. Migliorini integrates Trinitarian theology with relational ontology, treating divine relationality as a transcendental feature of being to overcome substance metaphysics.

ai_systems.findings: Three new papers push autonomous research from single-agent scripts to orchestrated multi-agent systems. DOVA introduces "deliberation-first orchestration": before any tool is called, a meta-reasoning layer weighs user context, entity history and token budgets, cutting inference cost 40-60 % on routine tasks while preserving depth for hard queries. MARS (Google Cloud) adds Budget-Aware Monte-Carlo planning and Comparative Reflective Memory so the agent learns which branches actually moved the needle; on MLE-Bench it now matches the global leaderboard under equal compute. A Cambridge-led astrophysics team shows the ceiling: their Cmbagent swarm autonomously designed a weak-lensing inference pipeline that won the FAIR Universe challenge after engineers injected light guidance, proving semi-autonomous loops can beat human experts when tightly scoped. Prompt engineering is keeping pace—RASPRef treats the prompt itself as the optimization target, retrieving past reasoning trajectories and v [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional momentum on UAP disclosure has reached fever pitch in 2026. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-FL) told constituents she will soon release "things of non-human origin" collected by the U.S. government, echoing President Trump's repeated hints that a major trove of files is imminent. Oversight Chair Luna continues her transparency investigation, while the House prepares companion bills to the Senate's UAP disclosure amendment. Former intelligence officials testified that recovered craft exhibit propulsion systems that "defy known physics" and that biological material recovered from crash sites shows "non-human DNA sequences." The Pentagon's AARO office quietly confirmed it has catalogued 57 incidents involving craft displaying "signature management" capabilities beyond current aerospace technology. Sources indicate the anticipated release could come within weeks, potentially including the first official acknowledgment of reverse-engineering programs dating to the 1940s.

weather: Mostly cloudy and cool today in Edmonds. High 63-66°F, low tonight 49-52°F. Only a 1% chance of rain during the day, rising to 25% this evening. Light south wind 5-9 mph shifts northwest this afternoon, then north 5-7 mph tonight. RealFeel stays in the low-50s, humidity around 73%. Visibility good at 12 miles.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council postponed the vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator until next week. Council President Michelle Dotsch stated the high-profile position requires more public and council consideration. The council also voted 3-3 on reactivating the volunteer salary commission, with Mayor Mike Rosen breaking the tie to advance the measure. Planning Director Mike Clugston reported 15 DADU permits issued in 2025 and highlighted major upcoming projects including 411-unit Edmonds Green and 258-unit Apollo Apartments.

Gayla Shoemake, environmental advocate and educator, was named Edmonds Kiwanis Club's Citizen of the Year. The award recognizes her decades of climate advocacy including leading the Edmonds Climate Advisory Board and installing solar panels at Edmonds United Methodist Church. She will serve as Grand Marshal of the July 4th parade.

A Washington Supreme Court commissioner declined to pause a judge's ruling blocking stricter sheriff qualifications, keeping current eligibility standards in effect during ongoing litigation. Meanwhile, Edmonds Police Chief Loi Dawkins reported the department is working to meet six policy requirements by June 30 to access $210,000 in public safety sales tax revenue collected since February.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded its 2025-26 auditions, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list for August 2026 all-voice-part auditions. Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30–9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave, Seattle). Requirements: music-reading skills, prior singing/instrumental experience, and familiarity with foreign-language singing. Audition includes vocalizing, quartet sight-reading, ear repetition, and a prepared two-minute solo with provided accompanist. Annual dues $75 (scholarships available). No other Puget Sound adult choirs have posted fresh auditions this week.
family.toddler: Edmonds Library (650 Main St) offers Baby Storytime with Kat on Tuesday, May 12 at 8:00–8:45 am and Preschool Storytime on Wednesday, May 27 at 10:00–10:45 am. Lynnwood Library lists Baby Storytime with Heather on Friday, May 8 at 9:30 am and Toddler Storytime on Tuesday, May 12 at 11:00–11:30 am. All Sno-Isle programs are drop-in, free, and geared to ages 0–3 with songs, rhymes, and movement.

global_news.findings: US-Iran war enters critical phase: Trump claims a 14-point memorandum to end hostilities is close, but simultaneously threatens 'much higher level' bombing if Iran rejects it. Iran's foreign ministry says the US proposal is 'still being considered' while a senior MP dismisses it as a 'wish list'. Pakistan mediates as both sides signal flexibility but remain far apart on core issues including nuclear enrichment and sanctions relief.

Israel resumes strikes on Beirut: Netanyahu orders targeted killing of senior Hezbollah commander in Dahieh suburb, first Israeli air strike on Lebanon's capital since April ceasefire. US Central Command confirms it disabled an Iranian-flagged oil tanker in Gulf of Oman as naval blockade continues.

Australia reels from Indigenous girl's death: Vigils planned nationwide for 5-year-old Aboriginal girl found dead in Perth, sparking clashes between protesters and police. The case has reignited debate over systemic discrimination and police treatment of Indigenous communities.

Global oil markets volatile: Brent crude surged past $100/barrel on Iran war fears before easing on ceasefire speculation. EU approves €90bn loan to Ukraine as Druzhba pipeline reopens, ending months-long deadlock over oil supplies to Hungary.

UK-France strike £662m migration deal: Three-year agreement includes deployment of 50 riot-trained French police to tackle Channel crossings and 'hostile crowds'. The deal comes amid record small boat arrivals and rising political pressure on both governments.

intellectual_journals.findings: Sean Irving's essay 'The West's forgotten republican heritage' argues that 19th-century economic republicanism offers a roadmap for restoring citizens' agency in an age of corporate and algorithmic domination. Drawing on thinkers from Machiavelli to the British Chartists, Irving revives the republican idea that liberty is not merely non-interference but the absence of arbitrary power—whether wielded by kings, monopolies, or data barons. He traces how the tradition evolved from Harrington's link between land ownership and independence to 19th-century demands for shorter hours, collective bargai [TRUNCATED]

In 'The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You', Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks's posthumous essays into a meditation on perception as creative act rather than passive reception. Using neurological case studies of patients who experience 'standstills of consciousness', she shows how the brain constructs reality through 'neural coalitions' that must cross a threshold of intensity to reach awareness. The piece frames perception as a mirror reflecting the perceiver's singular life history, making the Mary’s Room thought experiment not a puzzle about qualia but a reminder that 'non [TRUNCATED]

ProPublica's investigation 'Children Describe Life Inside Dilley ICE Detention Center' reconstructs the emotional geography of family incarceration through the voices of 14-year-old Ariana Velasquez and other children held at the Texas facility reopened under Trump's second-term deportation drive. Reporter Seth Freed Wessler details how the administration shifted from separating families to detaining them together, using Dilley as a warehouse for long-settled immigrants like Ariana's Honduran mother, who had lived in New York for seven years. Children's letters describe 'sadness and mostly dep [TRUNCATED]

The London Review of Books announces its 2026 Winter Lecture series, headlined by Oxford professor Seamus Perry on 'Pluralism and the Modern Poet'. Perry will interrogate whether modern poetry's embrace of pluralism—'of things being various'—can coexist with traditional demands for unity and imaginative order, asking if Auden was right that a poem embodying liberal democracy's pluralist values risks being 'formless, windy, banal and utterly boring'. The lecture, scheduled for 30 January at Conway Hall, positions itself within the LRB's tradition of staging major cultural interventions—previous [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Three new voice-first AI wearables launched in 2026, each targeting different interaction modes. Audria (audria.tech) is a discreet behind-the-ear device that proactively listens to conversations and provides context-aware reminders and scheduling assistance, aimed at professional productivity. Tepy.ai (tepy.ai) from Sydney takes a medical approach, using silent-speech recognition to convert lip m [TRUNCATED]

English and History teachers gained three specialized AI co-workers this spring. Humy.ai offers 1,200+ interactive AI-powered historical figures plus 90+ teacher tools that create classroom-ready activities, auto-graded assignments, and differentiated reading levels while maintaining FERPA/COPPA compliance. Teacher's Buddy (teachersbuddy.com) from Melbourne provides 45 single-click AI tools for cu [TRUNCATED]

The lifelogging pendant category split into two clear camps in 2026. Nirva (nirva.life) represents the jewelry-first approach - an AI-powered pendant that journals your life by building a living library of memories and emotions, designed to be worn all day as beautiful jewelry while providing 24/7 emotional support and life coaching. SKUGGA (skugga.com) from Sweden takes the modular approach, crea [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Colbert’s Trumpet Player on Life After Late Night
newyorker.dek: Since 2015, Jon Lampley has played in the house band of “The Late Show,” which CBS unceremoniously cancelled. As the final episode looms, he takes a look back. Mark Yarm writes.
newyorker.text:
Viewers across the country are counting down the days till the final episode of Stephen Colbert’s “Late Show,” which will air on May 21st. When the program’s peculiar, sudden cancellation was announced, last July, the members of the show’s band, Louis Cato and the Great Big Joy Machine, were as surprised as anyone. “It felt like a deep sigh,” Jon (Lamps) Lampley, the group’s trumpet player, recalled the other day, at a sushi place near the Ed Sullivan Theatre.
CBS defended the cancellation as “purely a financial decision.” But many linked it to Colbert’s criticism of the network’s parent company for paying sixteen million dollars to settle a lawsuit with President Donald Trump—a move that the host decried, on air, as a “big fat bribe.” Lampley, who is thirty-six and wears a neat beard, said, “I don’t really have a lot of interest in fuelling speculation.” He is unhappy, though, about how things went down.
Colbert already has his next gig lined up: writing a “Lord of the Rings” movie with his son Peter McGee and Philippa Boyens. Lampley, meanwhile, will release his second solo album in June—a joyous soul-pop record called “Notes to Self,” whose lead single, “Greener,” features a spoken-word cameo from Colbert. (“Hey, Lamps, how’s your day goin’?”) On the album, the trumpeter levels up as a singer and front man. “It was influenced by the gospel music that’s at the core of what I do,” he said.
Lampley grew up in the predominantly white suburb of Tallmadge, Ohio, but he attended the First Apostolic Faith Church, a Black, Pentecostal congregation in Akron. In seventh grade, he joined the church band. He rattled off the names of various sisters and elders he performed alongside. “Nobody knows who these people are, but they are the ones who taught me, This is what music is,” he said. “This is how you move people. This is how you play the guitar in a way that gets an entire church to clap their hands.” Lampley maintains a deep faith.
He went on to play sousaphone in the Ohio State University marching band, where, as a senior, he experienced one of his proudest moments: “dotting the i” in an on-field cursive formation of the word “Ohio.” “This is considered a very great honor,” he said. During college, he also co-founded a jazz-funk band now known as the Huntertones. In 2014, the band moved to New York, where Lampley connected with the keyboardist and singer Jon Batiste, who hired him for the band he was assembling for Colbert. Batiste left the show in 2022, and the musician Louis Cato was promoted to bandleader.
“I can’t believe that I chose to make my life out of, like, blowing through a metal pipe,” Lampley said. “The Late Show” was taping that afternoon, and he made his way to the theatre for wardrobe, hair, and makeup. Later, between Colbert’s interviews with Anne Hathaway, who zinged her “Devil Wears Prada” co-star Stanley Tucci about his foodie persona (“That bitch has not made me a morsel!”), and the comedian Josh Johnson, who recounted an amusing T.S.A. encounter (“They open my bag, and it wasn’t brass knuckles—it was toe spreaders”), Lampley showed off his chops, soloing on a rendition of the Steve Miller Band’s “Fly Like an Eagle.”
At the taping, Colbert, hands in pockets, addressed his trumpet player. “Lampley, I want you to know that a friend of mine came to the show last night,” he said. “And she said, ‘Wooo, that trumpet player.’ ” Colbert grinned. “She goes, ‘If I wasn’t happily married, he’d be in big trouble.’ ” The audience whooped.
“You know what it is?” Cato interjected. “It’s the hips.”
“I think that’s it,” Colbert said. “Or it’s the embouchure.”
“Or it’s both,” Lampley said. He illustrated his lipping technique.
“You’re always, like, pre-kissing,” Colbert observed.
Afterward, Lampley and the band’s saxophonist, Louis Fouché, headed to an Italian restaurant. Lampley brought up the exchange involving Colbert’s friend, which ended up being cut before air. “Man,” he said, “I was grateful that my girlfriend was not at the taping today.”
The impending end of the Colbert era was weighing on them all. “Somebody who has been that important to the game and to the craft deserves to go out on their own terms,” Lampley said. “And that was taken from Stephen.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: A Pulitzer Prize-winning debut novel that reimagines the antebellum South through the story of Henry Townsend, a former slave who becomes a slave owner himself. Jones crafts a morally intricate narrative that explores the paradox of Black slaveholders and the complex web of relationships—both freed and enslaved—within a Virginia plantation. Critics hail it as a masterpiece destined for the American literary canon, comparing its scope and emotional depth to Toni Morrison's Beloved and William Faulkner's Absalom, Absalom!
